insight: When deciding whether to kill enemy Engineers or Wild Drones early game, the decision is highly position-dependent. Killing Engineers can remove the opponent's option to buy drones and force them into defense, but this only matters if it actually denies them value; if they would buy the defense anyway, killing Engineers just makes them happy to spend their blue. The priority should be on whether you're actually constraining their choices.
author: mrguy888, amalloy, xyotsuba
context: Extended debate about early-game targeting priorities in low-inflation scenarios.

insight: Vivid Drone is most valuable when you have unspent Energy you wouldn't otherwise use, effectively converting excess Energy into extra Drones. It enables high-econ openers like 1EE 11 DDDD as Player 1, but such openings are vulnerable to rushing and should not be attempted in every Vivid Drone set.
author: amalloy, miuna6933
context: Discussion of Vivid Drone valuation and economy strategy.

insight: Drake provides value beyond being a fast attacker that generates Swords—it also techs you out of Blue by being a Green unit, which can be a significant strategic advantage by freeing up Blue-dependent purchases.
author: cactus_wren
context: New player discussing undervalued aspects of Drake.

insight: Plexo Cell is effective soak against Plasmafier, but only moderately. Plexo can repeatedly block Plasma attacks, but the Plasma player can choose to hold Plasmafier units to avoid wasting them. Early Plasmafier into Plexo creates a tempo swing where the Plasma player spends 12GGGB while Plexo player spends 2GG, initially favoring Plexo.
author: cogito3, swapgo, cachotognax
context: Analysis of Plasmafier vs Plexo Cell interaction and holding strategy.

insight: When holding absorbers like Omega Splitter instead of attacking, you gain tempo advantage because your opponent must preemptively buy defense for the Omega's attack before you would be forced to buy defense to handle their damage. This creates a 'half-turn efficiency' advantage in your favor.
author: f300xen, namington
context: Explanation of why holding absorbers is often superior to attacking.

insight: Megadrone absorption is slightly better than absorbing on Rhino, but absorbing on Rhino is considered poor play. Holding Megadrone is preferable to soaking with it defensively.
author: xujhan, xyotsuba
context: Brief comparison of absorption unit efficiency.

insight: Lucina is one of the best units in the game because she is an efficient cheap attacker whose ability both generates additional attack and defense while helping spend the overtech Red resources she generates. She is vulnerable during her tech window but builds her own defensive infrastructure.
author: amalloy, cogito3
context: Discussion of Lucina's strengths and how to build her efficiently.

insight: Cheap attack and efficient attack are not the same thing. Lucina is efficient but not the cheapest attacker, while Cybernetics is cheap but not efficient. Immolite produces the cheapest attack in base set.
author: mrguy888, cachotognax
context: Clarification of attack cost vs attack efficiency terminology.

insight: Shadowfang is best in sets with high economy and ideally a Red sink (like Combustible or Immolite). Without a sink, affording double Red and double Blue costs approximately 22-23 gold per turn, which is more drones than typical. Most big Red units want a strong absorber to protect them during their vulnerable tech period.
author: mrguy888, amalloy
context: Analysis of Shadowfang requirements and economy constraints.

insight: Against a Gauss Fabricator defense, you don't need to breach early. Instead, put sufficient pressure that your opponent must buy defense instead of attackers, allowing you to develop attackers yourself while they're locked into defense.
author: namington
context: Strategy for overcoming Gauss Fabricator-heavy defense.

insight: Xaetron is more effective as a blocker than as an attacker. Its clicking ability is inefficient (Tarsier-level damage output), but it provides maximum defensive value when you alternate absorbing between Xaetron and another unit. This alternation allows Xaetron's passive healing to generate 'extra' absorb compared to soaking only on Xaetron.
author: tarolg, amalloy, supergreyman
context: Extended analysis of Xaetron's optimal absorption pattern.

insight: When alternating absorbers between Xaetron and a simple blocker like Wall, you gain tempo advantage by buying defense slightly earlier on the Wall turns (since less passive healing happens), which is offset by having more tempo on Xaetron turns when it absorbs to near-death (1 HP). The net tempo swing favors the alternating strategy over always absorbing on one unit.
author: amalloy, xujhan
context: Analysis of tempo implications in alternating absorption patterns.

insight: In high-pressure scenarios, clicking Xaetron to attack may be inferior to using it as a pure defender because you cannot afford the tempo loss. Always absorbing on Xaetron (rather than alternating) is preferable under extreme pressure.
author: amalloy
context: Pressure-dependent evaluation of Xaetron's attacking role.

insight: Buying both Manimus and Scorchilla is inefficient. Each is good for converting Red when you don't want it anymore—use one or the other, not both together.
author: tarolg
context: Critique of redundant unit combinations.

insight: When your opponent goes second Animus, you must react immediately to a potential Lucina buy on the next turn. Delaying your defensive response by even one turn allows them to safely complete the Lucina transition.
author: cactus_wren, amalloy
context: Time-sensitive defensive play around Lucina tech.

insight: Grimbotch is typically bad to buy on turn 3 because it will just get absorbed without providing lasting value. It's better bought mid-game (turn 5+) when it can be soaked productively or when you have supporting attackers.
author: cactus_wren, leary93
context: Timing analysis for temporary attack units.

insight: Evil Animus (early Animus in Thorium sets) performs exceptionally well with Grimbotch or Feral Wither first because these cheap attackers exploit low-defense opponents and can transition into permanent attack more efficiently than the defending player can scale their defense.
author: leary93, swapgo
context: Named strategy pattern for early Animus aggression in specific sets.

insight: 'Timewalking' refers to invalidating your opponent's entire turn or action for minimal cost, essentially giving yourself an extra turn. Borrowed from Magic: The Gathering terminology.
author: swapgo, cogito3
context: Definition of common strategic terminology.

insight: When deciding whether to buy Redeemer, you must also buy sufficient soak to protect it. Buying Redeemer without adequate defense is generally a mistake. Synchronizing Gauss Cannon charges with Frostbite and other burst damage is more efficient than buying unprotected Redeemer.
author: supergreyman
context: Defensive requirements for purchasing expensive units.

insight: Fission Turret into Thermite is high-pressure strategy, but it's not always better than Gauss Cannon. Fission Turret has the advantage of costing less and providing a Green refund, while Gauss Cannon costs more but may be strictly better in certain positions. Do not build excessive Fission Turrets if they will die before you extract value.
author: cactus_wren, amalloy
context: Comparative analysis of pressure attackers and their cost efficiency.
